snapshot.stmt: Let \(Z\) and \(W\) be random elements, with \(W\) taking values in a standard-Borel space. There exists a probability kernel \(z\mapsto\kappa(z,\cdot)\) that is a regular conditional law of \(W\) given \(Z=z\), and it is unique for \(\operatorname{Law}(Z)\)-almost every \(z\).

node.statement: Let \(Z\) and \(W\) be random elements, with \(W\) taking values in a standard-Borel space. There exists a probability kernel \(z\mapsto\kappa(z,\cdot)\) that is a regular conditional law of \(W\) given \(Z=z\), and it is unique for \(\operatorname{Law}(Z)\)-almost every \(z\).

source.verbatim_statement: For any Borel space S and measurable space T, let ξ and η be random elements in S and T, respectively. Then there exists a probability kernel μ from T to S satisfying P[ξ ∈ · | η] = μ(η, ·) a.s., and μ is unique a.e. L(η).

attestation.note: Main verified this transcription against publicly indexed text of Kallenberg (2002), Theorem 6.3, cross-checked to the Springer source-of-record DOI. Under ξ=W and η=Z, it exactly supports the live lemma: only W needs a Borel state space, Z may use an arbitrary measurable state space, and uniqueness is Law(Z)-almost everywhere.

lem:tie-subexperiment-minimax-boundary.proof_tex:
We first verify membership and compute the target. The chosen study and treatment probabilities equal \(1/2\), so both positivity assumptions hold for every \(\varepsilon<1/2\). The covariate density is one. The endpoint law of \(Y\) is \(\nu_m\) with \(m=m_0+(b-a)u(x)\) in the cell \(S=1,A=0\) and \(m=m_0\) elsewhere. Choosing the radius of the \(C^\beta\)-ball for \(u\) sufficiently small keeps every mean in \([a,b]\) and every nuisance in the prescribed Hölder ball. Thus these laws form an admissible specialization of \(\overline{\mathcal P}\).

At \(\rho=\gamma=0\), support clipping is inactive because every conditional mean belongs to \([a,b]\), and the binary-treatment identity \(e_0(x,s)+e_1(x,s)=1\) gives\[C_t^-(x;0)=C_t^+(x;0)=\mu_{0t}(x),\quad V_t^-(x;0)=V_t^+(x;0)=\mu_{1t}(x),\quad W_t^-(x;0)=W_t^+(x;0)=\mu_{0t}(x).\]Consequently, the conditional violation for arm \(t\) is \(\lvert\mu_{1t}(x)-\mu_{0t}(x)\rvert\). Only arm zero differs in the submodel, whence\[C_{P_u}(0,0)=(b-a)\int_{[0,1]^d}|u(x)|\,dx.\]All four faces and the positive part tie on the whole covariate support at \(u=0\), so the tie has positive, indeed unit, \(P_X\)-mass.

We next prove the lower bound by an explicit mixture. Choose a nonzero nonnegative \(C^\infty\) function \(\psi\), supported in the interior of \([0,1]^d\), with \(\lVert\psi\rVert_\infty\le1\). For a small bandwidth \(h\), pack \(M\asymp h^{-d}\) disjoint cubes of side \(h\) into \([0,1]^d\), with lower corners \(x_1,\ldots,x_M\). For \(\omega=(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_M)\in\{-1,1\}^M\), set\[u_\omega(x)=c h^\beta\sum_{j=1}^M\omega_j\psi\!\left(\frac{x-x_j}{h}\right).\]The supports are disjoint. The scaling identity for derivatives and the Hölder remainder show directly that \(\lVert u_\omega\rVert_{C^\beta}\le C_\psi c\), uniformly in \(h\) and \(\omega\). Taking \(c\) small therefore places every alternative in the submodel and in \([-1/4,1/4]\). Moreover,\[\lVert u_\omega\rVert_1=c h^\beta M h^d\int|\psi(z)|\,dz\asymp c h^\beta.\]

Let \(L_\omega=dP_{u_\omega}/dP_0\) for one observation. Conditional on \(G=1\{S=1,A=0\}\), the Bernoulli likelihood ratio is \(1+2(2Z-1)u_\omega(X)\), and it is one when \(G=0\). Since \(P_0(G=1)=1/4\), \(E_{P_0}(2Z-1)=0\), and \((2Z-1)^2=1\), direct multiplication gives\[E_{P_0}[L_\omega L_{\omega'}]=1+\langle u_\omega,u_{\omega'}\rangle_2.\]Let \(\overline P_n=2^{-M}\sum_\omega P_{u_\omega}^{\otimes n}\). Orthogonality of the disjoint bumps gives, for a constant \(\lambda=c^2\int\psi^2\),\[\langle u_\omega,u_{\omega'}\rangle_2=\lambda h^{2\beta+d}\sum_{j=1}^M\omega_j\omega_j'.\]Using \((1+x)^n\le e^{nx}\), averaging independently over \(\omega\) and \(\omega'\), and using \(\log\cosh z\le z^2/2\), we obtain\[1+\chi^2(\overline P_n,P_0^{\otimes n})\le\left\{\cosh\bigl(n\lambda h^{2\beta+d}\bigr)\right\}^M\le\exp\left(\frac{M n^2\lambda^2h^{4\beta+2d}}2\right)\le\exp(Cc^4n^2h^{4\beta+d}).\]Take \(h\asymp n^{-2/(4\beta+d)}\). Then the last exponent is bounded by a constant multiple of \(c^4\), while every alternative has \(C_{P_{u_\omega}}(0,0)\asymp c(b-a)r_n\). By choosing \(c=c_\eta\) sufficiently small, the total-variation inequality \(\lVert\overline P_n-P_0^{\otimes n}\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}\le\sqrt{\chi^2(\overline P_n,P_0^{\otimes n})}/2\) makes this distance at most \(\eta+o(1)\). Therefore\[2^{-M}\sum_\omega E_{P_{u_\omega}}\varphi_n\le E_{P_0}\varphi_n+\eta+o(1)\le\alpha+\eta+o(1),\]so at least one mixture component has the asserted low power. If instead \(h\asymp n^{-1/(2\beta)}\), then the signal is of order \(n^{-1/2}\) and \(n^2h^{4\beta+d}=n^{-d/(2\beta)}\to0\). The same calculation gives total variation tending to zero and hence power at most \(\alpha+o(1)\) for at least one root-\(n\) alternative.

It remains to prove attainability of the rate. Define the projection kernel \(K_K(x,x')=\sum_{k=1}^Kp_k(x)p_k(x')\). The inverse-cell weight in \(R_i\) gives\[E_{P_u}[R_i\mid X_i=x]=4\left\{\frac14\left(\frac12+u(x)\right)-\frac14\left(\frac12\right)\right\}=u(x).\]Independence of two distinct observations therefore yields\[E_{P_u}[\widehat Q_K]=\sum_{k=1}^K\langle u,p_k\rangle_2^2=\lVert\Pi_Ku\rVert_2^2.\]A piecewise Taylor polynomial of degree \(\lfloor\beta\rfloor\) on cubes of side proportional to \(K^{-1/d}\) has pointwise remainder at most \(C K^{-\beta/d}\) by the defining Hölder inequality. Since the orthogonal projection is the best \(L_2\) approximation,\[\lVert u-\Pi_Ku\rVert_2\le C K^{-\beta/d}.\]Also \(|R_i|\le4\). Writing the order-two statistic as its first projection plus a degenerate remainder and expanding the variance gives\[\operatorname{Var}_{P_u}(\widehat Q_K)\le C\left\{\frac{\lVert\Pi_Ku\rVert_2^2}{n}+\frac{E[K_K(X_1,X_2)^2]}{n^2}\right\}=C\left\{\frac{\lVert\Pi_Ku\rVert_2^2}{n}+\frac K{n^2}\right\}.\]The equality uses orthonormality twice: \(E[K_K(X_1,X_2)^2]=K\). Under \(u=0\), Chebyshev's inequality thus makes the rejection probability at most \(C/L_\alpha^2\), so a fixed sufficiently large \(L_\alpha\) gives level at most \(\alpha\). On a unit-volume cube, \(\lVert u\rVert_2\ge\lVert u\rVert_1\). With \(K\asymp n^{2d/(4\beta+d)}\),\[K^{-\beta/d}\asymp r_n,\qquad \frac{\sqrt K}{n}\asymp r_n^2.\]Hence \(\lVert u\rVert_1/r_n\to\infty\) implies \(E\widehat Q_K/(\sqrt K/n)\to\infty\), and the displayed variance bound plus Chebyshev's inequality gives rejection probability tending to one uniformly. This proves the matching upper bound and the lemma.

snapshot.stmt: The primitive conditions admit the following specialization for every \(d\ge1\) and \(\beta>d/2\): take \(\mathcal X=[0,1]^d\), \(X\) uniform, \(S\) and \(A\) independent fair Bernoulli variables, and an interior baseline mean \(m_0=(a+b)/2\), with \(B\) and the density bounds chosen to contain this submodel. Put \(Z=(Y-a)/(b-a)\). For a function \(u:[0,1]^d\to[-1/4,1/4]\), let \(P_u\) have \(Z\) Bernoulli with conditional success probability \(1/2+u(X)\) when \(S=1,A=0\), and success probability \(1/2\) in every other study-arm cell. Restrict \(u\) to a fixed bounded \(C^\beta\)-ball small enough that the resulting outcome regressions lie in \(\mathcal H_B^\beta(\mathcal X)\). Then\[C_{P_u}(0,0)=(b-a)\lVert u\rVert_{L_1([0,1]^d)}.\]Let \(r_n=n^{-2\beta/(4\beta+d)}\). For every \(\eta>0\), there is \(c_\eta>0\) such that every test \(\varphi_n\) with \(E_{P_0}\varphi_n\le\alpha+o(1)\) has, for some \(P_{u_n}\),\[C_{P_{u_n}}(0,0)\asymp c_\eta r_n,\qquad E_{P_{u_n}}\varphi_n\le\alpha+\eta+o(1).\]There likewise exist alternatives with \(C_{P_{u_n}}(0,0)\asymp n^{-1/2}\) and power at most \(\alpha+o(1)\). Conversely, let \(p_1,\ldots,p_K\) be an orthonormal piecewise-polynomial basis of order at least \(\lceil\beta\rceil\), put \(K\asymp n^{2d/(4\beta+d)}\), define \[R_i=4\{1\{S_i=1,A_i=0\}-1\{S_i=0,A_i=0\}\}Z_i,\qquad \widehat Q_K=\frac{1}{n(n-1)}\sum_{i\ne j}R_iR_j\sum_{k=1}^Kp_k(X_i)p_k(X_j),\]and reject for \(\widehat Q_K>L_\alpha\sqrt K/n\), where \(L_\alpha\) is a sufficiently large constant. This test has level at most \(\alpha\) under \(P_0\) and power tending to one uniformly over the displayed Hölder submodel whenever \(C_{P_u}(0,0)/r_n\to\infty\). Thus \(r_n\) is the sharp minimax separation order in this positive-mass arm-and-endpoint-tie subexperiment.

assumptions.ass:density-bounds: \(\underline f\le f_X(x)\le\bar f\) for Lebesgue-almost every \(x\in\mathcal X\).
assumptions.ass:uniform-treatment-positivity: \(\varepsilon\le e_t(X,s)\le1-\varepsilon\) \(P\)-almost surely for every \(t\in\mathcal T\) and \(s\in\{0,1\}\).
assumptions.ass:holder-nuisances: \(\pi\in\mathcal H_B^\beta(\mathcal X)\), \(e_t(\cdot,s)\in\mathcal H_B^\beta(\mathcal X)\), and \(\mu_{st}\in\mathcal H_B^\beta(\mathcal X)\) for every \(t\in\mathcal T\) and \(s\in\{0,1\}\).

lem:regular-constant-path-power.proof_tex: The target calculation in \(\textup{lem:tie-subexperiment-minimax-boundary}\) gives\[C_{P_{u_n}}(0,0)=(b-a)|u_n|=\frac{|\kappa|}{\sqrt n}.\]Let \(G_1=1\{S=1,A=0\}\) and \(G_0=1\{S=0,A=0\}\). Since both cells have probability \(1/4\),\[E_{P_{u_n}}D_i=4(b-a)\left\{\frac14u_n-\frac14\cdot0\right\}=\frac{\kappa}{\sqrt n}.\]Because \(Z_i\in\{0,1\}\), \((Z_i-1/2)^2=1/4\) identically. The cells are disjoint and have total probability \(1/2\), so\[E_{P_{u_n}}D_i^2=16(b-a)^2\cdot\frac12\cdot\frac14=2(b-a)^2=\sigma^2.\]Thus \(\operatorname{Var}_{P_{u_n}}(D_i)\to\sigma^2\), and \(|D_i|\le2(b-a)\). For completeness, write \(X_{ni}=(D_i-E D_i)/(\sigma\sqrt n)\). Uniform boundedness gives \(\max_i|X_{ni}|\to0\), while \(\sum_iE X_{ni}^2\to1\). Taylor expansion \(E e^{itX_{ni}}=1-t^2E[X_{ni}^2]/2+o(E[X_{ni}^2])\), uniformly in \(i\), and multiplication over the independent observations show that the characteristic function of \(\sum_iX_{ni}\) converges to \(e^{-t^2/2}\). Therefore\[\frac{\sqrt n}{\sigma}\left(\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^nD_i-\frac{\kappa}{\sqrt n}\right)\rightsquigarrow N(0,1),\]which is the asserted shifted-normal limit. Taking the probability of the event \(|Z+\kappa/\sigma|>z_{1-\alpha/2}\) for \(Z\sim N(0,1)\) yields the displayed power formula.

snapshot.stmt: In the canonical subexperiment of \(\textup{lem:tie-subexperiment-minimax-boundary}\), fix \(\kappa\in\mathbb R\) and take \(u_n(x)=\kappa/[(b-a)\sqrt n]\). Then \(C_{P_{u_n}}(0,0)=|\kappa|/\sqrt n\). Define\[D_i=4(b-a)\{1\{S_i=1,A_i=0\}-1\{S_i=0,A_i=0\}\}\left(Z_i-\frac12\right),\qquad \sigma^2=2(b-a)^2.\]Under \(P_{u_n}\), \(\sqrt n\,n^{-1}\sum_iD_i/\sigma\) converges to \(N(\kappa/\sigma,1)\). If \(\Phi\) is the standard-normal distribution function and \(z_p=\Phi^{-1}(p)\), the two-sided level-\(\alpha\) test rejecting when the absolute standardized statistic exceeds \(z_{1-\alpha/2}\) has limiting power\[1-\Phi\!\left(z_{1-\alpha/2}-\frac{\kappa}{\sigma}\right)+\Phi\!\left(-z_{1-\alpha/2}-\frac{\kappa}{\sigma}\right).\]Thus fixed regular directions have the pointwise \(n^{-1/2}\) boundary even though the uniform tie-inclusive boundary in \(\textup{lem:tie-subexperiment-minimax-boundary}\) is slower.

thm:tie-local-separation-obstruction.proof_tex:
The specialization constructed in \(\textup{lem:tie-subexperiment-minimax-boundary}\) is contained in \(\overline{\mathcal P}\), and \(P_0\) satisfies \(C_{P_0}(0,0)=0\). Hence any sequence of tests obeying\[\sup_{P:C_P(0,0)=0}E_P[\varphi_n]\le\alpha+o(1)\]also obeys \(E_{P_0}[\varphi_n]\le\alpha+o(1)\). Applying the lower-bound part of \(\textup{lem:tie-subexperiment-minimax-boundary}\), for every \(\eta>0\) there is an alternative \(P_{u_n}\) in that subexperiment such that\[C_{P_{u_n}}(0,0)\asymp c_\eta n^{-2\beta/(4\beta+d)},\qquad E_{P_{u_n}}[\varphi_n]\le\alpha+\eta+o(1).\]The same lemma's second mixture choice produces alternatives satisfying \(C_{P_{u_n}}(0,0)\asymp n^{-1/2}\) and \(E_{P_{u_n}}[\varphi_n]\le\alpha+o(1)\). Thus a positive-mass tie admits root-\(n\) alternatives asymptotically indistinguishable from the null in worst case. Its matching upper-bound calculation shows, within this canonical subexperiment, that the explicit quadratic projection test is consistent whenever\[\frac{C_{P_u}(0,0)}{n^{-2\beta/(4\beta+d)}}\longrightarrow\infty.\]This establishes the asserted sharp boundary only for the stated subexperiment.

For the contrasting constant directions, \(\textup{lem:regular-constant-path-power}\) gives \(C_{P_{u_n}}(0,0)=|\kappa|/\sqrt n\) and a standardized limiting distribution \(N(\kappa/\sigma,1)\), where \(\sigma=\sqrt2(b-a)\). If a uniformly valid test had a nontrivial worst-case power curve determined only by \(\sqrt n\,C_P(\theta)\), the root-\(n\) alternatives from the first paragraph would force that curve to exceed its null level at a fixed positive local separation. Their power is instead at most \(\alpha+o(1)\), which is a contradiction. This argument applies to any inversion of the numerical directional-delta procedure only conditionally on that inversion having the claimed uniform level. Nothing in either supporting lemma establishes multiplier critical-value validity, confidence-region coverage, or uniform level for that procedure. The coverage and level questions therefore remain undecided, exactly as stated.

defs.def:inference-handle: Use a normalized tensor-product spline or compactly supported wavelet basis \(p_K(x)\) of order at least \(\lceil\beta\rceil\), with \(K=K_n\), and fixed \(J\)-folds. On training complements fit truncated series logits \(\widehat\pi^{(-j)},\widehat e_k^{(-j)}\) and bounded series least squares \(\widehat\mu_{sk}^{(-j)}\). Require for this construction \(K_n\log n/n=o(1)\), \(K_n^{-\beta/d}=o(n^{-1/4})\), foldwise uniform errors \(r_{\pi,n},r_{e,n},r_{\mu,n}\), and \(r_{\mu,n}(r_{e,n}+r_{\pi,n})=o(n^{-1/2})\). Put \(p_s(x)=\pi(x)^s[1-\pi(x)]^{1-s}\), \(R^\mu_{sk}=1\{S=s,A=k\}[Y-\mu_{sk}(X)]/[p_s(X)e_k(X,s)]\), and \(R^e_{sk}=1\{S=s\}[1\{A=k\}-e_k(X,s)]/p_s(X)\). For any face \(h\in\{V_t^-,V_t^+,W_t^-,W_t^+\}\), define \(\psi_h(O;\theta)=h(X;\theta)-E_Ph(X;\theta)+\sum_{s,k}\partial_{\mu_{sk}}h(X;\theta)R^\mu_{sk}+\sum_{s,k}\partial_{e_k(\cdot,s)}h(X;\theta)R^e_{sk}\), with branchwise directional derivatives of the displayed clipped formula; the \(\pi\)-derivative is zero. Let \(\mathcal S_n^{\mathrm{act}}\) collect centered face scores attaining a lower maximum, upper minimum, positive-part contact, or outer arm maximum at every \(\theta\in\Theta_n\), retaining all ties. Its sufficient performance conditions are an envelope \(F\) with \(\sup_{P\in\overline{\mathcal P}}E_PF^q<\infty\) for some \(q>4\), \(\log N(\eta,\mathcal S_n^{\mathrm{act}},L_2(P))\le v\log(A_0/\eta)\) uniformly in \(P\) and \(0<\eta\le1\), and a fixed studentization floor for nonzero-variance scores; zero-variance scores are deterministically zero. Let \(\widehat C_n(\theta)\) be the cross-fitted empirical face vector passed through the lower-max, upper-min, positive-part, expectation, and outer-max map \(\mathfrak F\). With independent mean-zero unit-variance multipliers, put \(\mathbb G_n^\xi=n^{-1/2}\sum_i\xi_i\widehat\psi_i\), choose \(\delta_n\downarrow0\) with \(n^{-1/2}/\delta_n\to0\) and \((r_{\pi,n}+r_{e,n}+r_{\mu,n})/\delta_n\to0\), and set \(\widehat C_n^\xi=[\mathfrak F(\widehat\eta_n+\delta_n\mathbb G_n^\xi)-\mathfrak F(\widehat\eta_n)]/\delta_n\). Take deterministic \(\Theta_n\subset[0,1]^2\) with mesh \(h_n\to0\) and interpolation error \(o(n^{-1/2})\). Let \(\widehat c_{1-\alpha}\) be the conditional \((1-\alpha)\)-quantile of \(\sup_{\theta\in\Theta_n}\widehat C_n^\xi(\theta)\), and define \(\widehat{\mathcal K}_{n,1-\alpha}=\{\theta\in\Theta_n:\sqrt n\widehat C_n(\theta)\le\widehat c_{1-\alpha}\}\), enlarged by measurable closed grid cells in \(\Theta\). Hong--Li supplies the finite-difference derivative, Fang--Santos the directional map, Chernozhukov--Lee--Rosen contact selection, and cross-fitting nuisance linearization.

The specialization constructed in \(\textup{lem:tie-subexperiment-minimax-boundary}\) is contained in \(\overline{\mathcal P}\), and \(P_0\) satisfies \(C_{P_0}(0,0)=0\). thm:exact-compatibility-characterization.proof_tex:
Fix \(\theta=(\rho,\gamma)\). We prove necessity and sufficiency separately.

\emph{Necessity.} Let \(Q\in\mathcal M(P,\rho,\gamma)\). By the pushforward restriction, on an observational arm with positive conditional probability,\[E_Q\{Y(t)\mid X,S=0,A=t\}=E_P\{Y\mid X,S=0,A=t\}=\mu_{0t}(X).\]By definition,\[E_Q\{Y(t)\mid X,S=0,A=1-t\}=c_t(X).\]Conditioning first on \(A\) therefore gives\[q_{t0}(X)=e_t(X,0)\mu_{0t}(X)+e_{1-t}(X,0)c_t(X)\qquad P_{X\mid S=0}\text{-almost surely}.\]The study-positivity inequality \(1-\pi(X)\ge\varepsilon\) makes \(P_{X\mid S=0}\) and \(P_X\) equivalent. Hence the last display holds \(P_X\)-almost surely. This argument also shows explicitly why division by observational-study mass is required.

The relative-NUC restriction and the nonnegativity of the treatment probabilities imply\[\begin{aligned}e_t(X,0)\mu_{0t}(X)+e_{1-t}(X,0)C_t^-(X;\rho)&\le q_{t0}(X)\\&\le e_t(X,0)\mu_{0t}(X)+e_{1-t}(X,0)C_t^+(X;\rho).\end{aligned}\]By the definition of the exact endpoints, this is\[q_{t0}(X)\in[W_t^-(X;\rho),W_t^+(X;\rho)]\qquad P_X\text{-almost surely}.\]Study exchangeability also gives\[q_{t0}(X)\in[V_t^-(X;\gamma),V_t^+(X;\gamma)]\qquad P_X\text{-almost surely}.\]Thus the two closed intervals intersect almost surely, so\[\max\{V_t^-(X;\gamma),W_t^-(X;\rho)\}\le\min\{V_t^+(X;\gamma),W_t^+(X;\rho)\}\]for each \(t\). Every positive-part violation is zero almost surely, and therefore \(C_P(\theta)=0\).

\emph{Sufficiency.} Suppose \(C_P(\theta)=0\), and put\[\ell_t(x)=\max\{V_t^-(x;\gamma),W_t^-(x;\rho)\},\qquad u_t(x)=\min\{V_t^+(x;\gamma),W_t^+(x;\rho)\}.\]For \(D_t(X)=(\ell_t(X)-u_t(X))_+\), the definition of \(C_P\) yields \(E_{P_X}D_t=0\) for every \(t\), because all of these expectations are nonnegative and their finite maximum is zero. Hence \(D_t=0\) almost surely. On the resulting common full-measure set, define\[q_t^*(x)=\frac{\ell_t(x)+u_t(x)}2.\]Then\[q_t^*(x)\in[V_t^-(x;\gamma),V_t^+(x;\gamma)]\cap[W_t^-(x;\rho),W_t^+(x;\rho)].\]Since \(e_{1-t}(x,0)\ge\varepsilon\), set\[c_t^*(x)=\frac{q_t^*(x)-e_t(x,0)\mu_{0t}(x)}{e_{1-t}(x,0)}.\]Membership of \(q_t^*\) in the \(W_t\)-interval and division by the positive coefficient \(e_{1-t}(x,0)\) give\[C_t^-(x;\rho)\le c_t^*(x)\le C_t^+(x;\rho).\]In particular, \(c_t^*(x)\in[a,b]\). All displayed selectors are measurable because they are obtained from measurable nuisance versions by finitely many maxima, minima, sums, and division by a function bounded away from zero. Modify them on the exceptional \(P_X\)-null set, if necessary, to take values in \([a,b]\).

It remains to construct one common latent law rather than merely compatible conditional means. By Lemma \(\textup{lem:standard-borel-conditional-kernel}\), choose measurable kernels \(\kappa_{st}(x,dy)\) that are versions of the conditional law of \(Y\) given \(X=x,S=s,A=t\). Positivity ensures\[\int y\,\kappa_{st}(x,dy)=\mu_{st}(x)\qquad P_X\text{-almost surely}.\]Write \(p_1(x)=\pi(x)\) and \(p_0(x)=1-\pi(x)\). The observed law then has the disintegration\[P(dx,\{s\},\{k\},dy)=P_X(dx)p_s(x)e_k(x,s)\kappa_{sk}(x,dy).\]

For \(m\in[a,b]\), the endpoint kernel satisfies\[\int y\,\nu_m(dy)=a\frac{b-m}{b-a}+b\frac{m-a}{b-a}=m.\]Moreover, \(m\mapsto\nu_m(B)\) is Borel measurable for every Borel \(B\subseteq[a,b]\), so it is a probability kernel. Define conditional laws of the potential-outcome pair by\[\begin{aligned}\Lambda_{1k}(x,dy_0,dy_1)&=\kappa_{10}(x,dy_0)\kappa_{11}(x,dy_1),\qquad k\in\{0,1\},\\\Lambda_{00}(x,dy_0,dy_1)&=\kappa_{00}(x,dy_0)\nu_{c_1^*(x)}(dy_1),\\\Lambda_{01}(x,dy_0,dy_1)&=\nu_{c_0^*(x)}(dy_0)\kappa_{01}(x,dy_1).\end{aligned}\]These are measurable probability kernels. Now define \(Q^*\) on \((X,S,A,Y(0),Y(1))\) by\[Q^*(dx,\{s\},\{k\},dy_0,dy_1)=P_X(dx)p_s(x)e_k(x,s)\Lambda_{sk}(x,dy_0,dy_1).\]

For every \(s,k\), the \(k\)-th marginal of \(\Lambda_{sk}\) is \(\kappa_{sk}\). Consequently, the law of \((X,S,A,Y(A))\) under \(Q^*\) is exactly \(P\), proving the pushforward restriction. In study one, \(\Lambda_{1k}=\kappa_{10}\otimes\kappa_{11}\) does not depend on \(k\); hence\[(Y(0),Y(1))\perp_{Q^*}A\mid(X,S=1).\]In study zero, treatment positivity and the mean identity for \(\nu_m\) give\[E_{Q^*}\{Y(t)\mid X,S=0,A=1-t\}=c_t^*(X).\]Thus relative NUC holds. A direct conditioning calculation gives\[\begin{aligned}E_{Q^*}\{Y(t)\mid X,S=0\}&=e_t(X,0)\mu_{0t}(X)+e_{1-t}(X,0)c_t^*(X)\\&=q_t^*(X),\end{aligned}\]and membership of \(q_t^*\) in the \(V_t\)-interval proves study exchangeability. Hence \(Q^*\in\mathcal M(P,\rho,\gamma)\), establishing sufficiency.

The two directions prove the equivalence. The displayed equality for \(\mathcal K(P)\) follows from its definition as the zero set of \(C_P\). At no step was a unique active face required, so endpoint ties, lower-maximum ties, upper-minimum ties, and outer-arm ties are all included.

snapshot.stmt: Suppose \(\textup{ass:uniform-study-positivity}\) and \(\textup{ass:uniform-treatment-positivity}\) hold. Then, for every \(\theta=(\rho,\gamma)\in[0,1]^2\),\[\mathcal M(P,\rho,\gamma)\ne\varnothing \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad C_P(\theta)=0.\]Consequently, the exact observed-law compatibility region is the computable zero set\[\mathcal K(P)=\{\theta\in[0,1]^2:\mathcal M(P,\rho,\gamma)\ne\varnothing\}.\]The equivalence allows arbitrary positive-\(P_X\)-mass ties among the clipped lower and upper faces.

defs.def:selector-extension-handle: For each \(t\in\mathcal T\), set \(q_t^\star(x;\theta)=[\max\{V_t^-(x;\gamma),W_t^-(x;\rho)\}+\min\{V_t^+(x;\gamma),W_t^+(x;\rho)\}]/2\) and \(c_t^\star(x;\theta)=[q_t^\star(x;\theta)-e_t(x,0)\mu_{0t}(x)]/e_{1-t}(x,0)\). The construction route copies the observed trial arm-\(t\) outcome kernel to \(Y(t)\) under both trial assignments and, in study zero, uses \(\nu_{c_t^\star(X)}\) for each missing potential outcome, conditionally independently given \(X,S,A\).

lem:scalar-face-tangent-nonfactorization.proof_tex:
Let \(P_0\) be the null law in the canonical subexperiment and fix \(\theta_0=(0,0)\). The baseline mean \(m_0=(a+b)/2\) is in the interior of the outcome support. Both \(r_0\) and \(r_1\) are bounded smooth functions, with \(|r_1|\le 1/2\). Therefore, for all sufficiently small \(|\tau|\), the perturbed mean \(m_0+\tau r_j(x)\) remains in \([a,b]\), the corresponding endpoint outcome kernel is valid, and the perturbed regression remains in the fixed Hölder submodel admitted by \(\textup{lem:tie-subexperiment-minimax-boundary}\). Thus \(P_{\tau,r_j}\in\overline{\mathcal P}\).

Write \(u_{\tau,r}(x)=\tau r(x)/(b-a)\). This is exactly the parametrization of the trial cell \(S=1,A=0\) used in \(\textup{lem:tie-subexperiment-minimax-boundary}\), since its conditional outcome mean is
\[m_0+(b-a)u_{\tau,r}(x)=m_0+\tau r(x).\]
The target identity proved in that lemma therefore gives
\[C_{P_{\tau,r}}(0,0)=(b-a)\lVert u_{\tau,r}\rVert_1=|\tau|\int_{[0,1]^d}|r(x)|\,dx.\]
Taking the right derivative at zero yields
\[\dot C_0(r):=\left.\frac{d}{d\tau}C_{P_{\tau,r}}(0,0)\right|_{\tau=0+}=\int_{[0,1]^d}|r(x)|\,dx.\]
Consequently \(\dot C_0(r_0)=0\). Since \(X\) is uniform on \([0,1]^d\), Fubini's identity gives
\[\dot C_0(r_1)=\int_0^1|z-1/2|\,dz=2\int_0^{1/2}(1/2-z)\,dz=\frac14.\]

It remains to compare the information retained by the integrated face coordinates. At \(\rho=\gamma=0\), clipping is inactive in this interior submodel, and \(\textup{def:exact-endpoints}\) gives
\[V_t^-=V_t^+=\mu_{1t},\qquad W_t^-=W_t^+=\mu_{0t}.\]
Along \(P_{\tau,r}\), only \(\mu_{10}\) changes. Hence the only possibly nonzero integrated face derivatives are those of \(E[V_0^-]\) and \(E[V_0^+]\), and both equal
\[\left.\frac{d}{d\tau}E_{P_X}[m_0+\tau r(X)]\right|_{\tau=0}=E_{P_X}[r(X)].\]
For \(r_0\) this expectation is zero. For \(r_1(x)=x_1-1/2\), it is also zero because \(X_1\) is uniform on \([0,1]\). Every other integrated face derivative is identically zero along both paths. Thus the two paths have the same integrated face-derivative vector but distinct compatibility derivatives. No map whose argument is only that vector can represent \(\dot C_0\).

Finally, \(\textup{def:inference-handle}\) defines each \(\psi_h(O;\theta)\) as a scalar score and \(\mathbb G_n^\xi=n^{-1/2}\sum_i\xi_i\widehat\psi_i\) as the resulting scalar vector over faces and sensitivity indices. The preceding non-factorization shows algebraically that this vector alone omits the covariate-indexed direction on which the pointwise positive part depends. Retaining all scalar face ties does not restore the lost function \(x\mapsto r(x)\). This proves the lemma.

snapshot.stmt: In the canonical positive-mass-tie subexperiment of \(\textup{lem:tie-subexperiment-minimax-boundary}\), let \(P_{\tau,r}\) agree with \(P_0\) except that \(\mu_{10,\tau}(x)=m_0+\tau r(x)\), where \(r\) is bounded and belongs to the admitted \(C^\beta\)-ball. For all sufficiently small \(|\tau|\), \[C_{P_{\tau,r}}(0,0)=|\tau|\int_{[0,1]^d}|r(x)|\,dx.\] Nevertheless, the two directions \(r_0(x)=0\) and \(r_1(x)=x_1-1/2\) have the same zero derivative at \(\tau=0\) for every integrated exact-face coordinate \(E[V_t^-]\), \(E[V_t^+]\), \(E[W_t^-]\), and \(E[W_t^+]\), whereas \[\left.\frac{d}{d\tau}C_{P_{\tau,r_0}}(0,0)\right|_{\tau=0+}=0,\qquad \left.\frac{d}{d\tau}C_{P_{\tau,r_1}}(0,0)\right|_{\tau=0+}=\frac14.\] Hence the directional derivative of the compatibility functional at this positive-mass tie does not factor through the vector of integrated face derivatives. In particular, the scalar face-score multiplier vector displayed in \(\textup{def:inference-handle}\) does not by itself specify the selector-complete, covariate-indexed tangent needed to differentiate the pointwise max--min and positive-part operations before integration.

defs.def:inference-handle: Use a normalized tensor-product spline or compactly supported wavelet basis \(p_K(x)\) of order at least \(\lceil\beta\rceil\), with \(K=K_n\), and fixed \(J\)-folds. On training complements fit truncated series logits \(\widehat\pi^{(-j)},\widehat e_k^{(-j)}\) and bounded series least squares \(\widehat\mu_{sk}^{(-j)}\). Require for this construction \(K_n\log n/n=o(1)\), \(K_n^{-\beta/d}=o(n^{-1/4})\), foldwise uniform errors \(r_{\pi,n},r_{e,n},r_{\mu,n}\), and \(r_{\mu,n}(r_{e,n}+r_{\pi,n})=o(n^{-1/2})\). Put \(p_s(x)=\pi(x)^s[1-\pi(x)]^{1-s}\), \(R^\mu_{sk}=1\{S=s,A=k\}[Y-\mu_{sk}(X)]/[p_s(X)e_k(X,s)]\), and \(R^e_{sk}=1\{S=s\}[1\{A=k\}-e_k(X,s)]/p_s(X)\). For any face \(h\in\{V_t^-,V_t^+,W_t^-,W_t^+\}\), define \(\psi_h(O;\theta)=h(X;\theta)-E_Ph(X;\theta)+\sum_{s,k}\partial_{\mu_{sk}}h(X;\theta)R^\mu_{sk}+\sum_{s,k}\partial_{e_k(\cdot,s)}h(X;\theta)R^e_{sk}\), with branchwise directional derivatives of the displayed clipped formula; the \(\pi\)-derivative is zero. Let \(\mathcal S_n^{\mathrm{act}}\) collect centered face scores attaining a lower maximum, upper minimum, positive-part contact, or outer arm maximum at every \(\theta\in\Theta_n\), retaining all ties. Its sufficient performance conditions are an envelope \(F\) with \(\sup_{P\in\overline{\mathcal P}}E_PF^q<\infty\) for some \(q>4\), \(\log N(\eta,\mathcal S_n^{\mathrm{act}},L_2(P))\le v\log(A_0/\eta)\) uniformly in \(P\) and \(0<\eta\le1\), and a fixed studentization floor for nonzero-variance scores; zero-variance scores are deterministically zero. Let \(\widehat C_n(\theta)\) be the cross-fitted empirical face vector passed through the lower-max, upper-min, positive-part, expectation, and outer-max map \(\mathfrak F\). With independent mean-zero unit-variance multipliers, put \(\mathbb G_n^\xi=n^{-1/2}\sum_i\xi_i\widehat\psi_i\), choose \(\delta_n\downarrow0\) with \(n^{-1/2}/\delta_n\to0\) and \((r_{\pi,n}+r_{e,n}+r_{\mu,n})/\delta_n\to0\), and set \(\widehat C_n^\xi=[\mathfrak F(\widehat\eta_n+\delta_n\mathbb G_n^\xi)-\mathfrak F(\widehat\eta_n)]/\delta_n\). Take deterministic \(\Theta_n\subset[0,1]^2\) with mesh \(h_n\to0\) and interpolation error \(o(n^{-1/2})\). Let \(\widehat c_{1-\alpha}\) be the conditional \((1-\alpha)\)-quantile of \(\sup_{\theta\in\Theta_n}\widehat C_n^\xi(\theta)\), and define \(\widehat{\mathcal K}_{n,1-\alpha}=\{\theta\in\Theta_n:\sqrt n\widehat C_n(\theta)\le\widehat c_{1-\alpha}\}\), enlarged by measurable closed grid cells in \(\Theta\). Hong--Li supplies the finite-difference derivative, Fang--Santos the directional map, Chernozhukov--Lee--Rosen contact selection, and cross-fitting nuisance linearization.

assumptions.ass:density-bounds: \(\underline f\le f_X(x)\le\bar f\) for Lebesgue-almost every \(x\in\mathcal X\).
assumptions.ass:uniform-treatment-positivity: \(\varepsilon\le e_t(X,s)\le1-\varepsilon\) \(P\)-almost surely for every \(t\in\mathcal T\) and \(s\in\{0,1\}\).
assumptions.ass:holder-nuisances: \(\pi\in\mathcal H_B^\beta(\mathcal X)\), \(e_t(\cdot,s)\in\mathcal H_B^\beta(\mathcal X)\), and \(\mu_{st}\in\mathcal H_B^\beta(\mathcal X)\) for every \(t\in\mathcal T\) and \(s\in\{0,1\}\).

lem:observational-mixture-identity.proof_tex: Fix \(t\in\mathcal T\), and define the conditional discrepancy\[D_t(x)=q_{t0}(x)-e_t(x,0)\mu_{0t}(x)-e_{1-t}(x,0)c_t(x).\]All conditional expectations may be represented by arbitrary measurable versions. Multiplication by the observational-study probability makes the asserted equality invariant to the values of those versions outside the support of \(X\mid S=0\). By conditional iterated expectation under \(Q\), and because \(A\in\{t,1-t\}\),\[\begin{aligned}q_{t0}(X)&=E_Q[Y(t)\mid X,S=0]\\&=Q(A=t\mid X,S=0)E_Q[Y(t)\mid X,S=0,A=t]\\&\quad+Q(A=1-t\mid X,S=0)E_Q[Y(t)\mid X,S=0,A=1-t]\end{aligned}\]almost surely on \(\{S=0\}\). Assumption \(\textup{ass:pushforward}\) identifies the two conditional assignment probabilities with \(e_t(X,0)\) and \(e_{1-t}(X,0)\), respectively. On \(\{S=0,A=t\}\), consistency of the realized outcome gives \(Y(t)=Y(A)\), and the same pushforward assumption identifies the corresponding conditional mean with \(\mu_{0t}(X)\). The other conditional mean is \(c_t(X)\) by definition. Hence \(D_t(X)=0\) \(Q\)-almost surely on \(\{S=0\}\). The pushforward also gives \(Q(S=0\mid X)=P(S=0\mid X)=1-\pi(X)\). Therefore\[E_{P_X}[(1-\pi(X))|D_t(X)|]=E_Q[1\{S=0\}|D_t(X)|]=0.\]The integrand is nonnegative, so \((1-\pi(X))D_t(X)=0\) \(P_X\)-almost surely. On \(\{x:\pi(x)<1\}\), the factor \(1-\pi(x)\) is strictly positive, and division yields \(D_t(x)=0\) \(P_X\)-almost surely there. Since \(t\) was arbitrary and \(\mathcal T\) is finite, the asserted conclusion holds for every \(t\in\mathcal T\).

snapshot.stmt: Every \(Q\) satisfying \(\textup{ass:pushforward}\) obeys the weighted observational-mixture identity\[(1-\pi(X))\bigl[q_{t0}(X)-e_t(X,0)\mu_{0t}(X)-e_{1-t}(X,0)c_t(X)\bigr]=0\]\(P_X\)-almost surely for each \(t\in\mathcal T\). Consequently, the unweighted identity holds \(P_X\)-almost surely on \(\{x:\pi(x)<1\}\).

prop:positive-violation-infeasibility.proof_tex: Suppose, toward a contradiction, that \(C_P(\theta)>0\) and that some \(Q\in\mathcal M(P,\rho,\gamma)\) exists. By \(\textup{def:latent-class}\), this law satisfies \(\textup{ass:pushforward}\), \(\textup{ass:relative-nuc}\), and \(\textup{ass:study-exchangeability}\). By \(\textup{lem:observational-mixture-identity}\), for each \(t\in\mathcal T\),\[(1-\pi(X))\{q_{t0}(X)-e_t(X,0)\mu_{0t}(X)-e_{1-t}(X,0)c_t(X)\}=0\]almost surely. Assumption \(\textup{ass:uniform-study-positivity}\) gives \(1-\pi(X)\ge\varepsilon>0\), so division yields\[q_{t0}(X)=e_t(X,0)\mu_{0t}(X)+e_{1-t}(X,0)c_t(X)\qquad P_X\text{-almost surely}.\]Moreover, \(\textup{ass:uniform-study-positivity}\) and \(\textup{ass:uniform-treatment-positivity}\) imply\[P(S=0,A=k\mid X)=(1-\pi(X))e_k(X,0)\ge\varepsilon^2\]for every \(k\in\mathcal T\), so the arm-specific conditional means are defined on the relevant \(P_X\)-support. The binary-treatment probabilities are nonnegative and sum to one. Assumption \(\textup{ass:relative-nuc}\), together with \(\textup{def:exact-endpoints}\), therefore implies endpoint by endpoint that\[W_t^-(X;\rho)\le q_{t0}(X)\le W_t^+(X;\rho)\qquad P_X\text{-almost surely}.\]Assumption \(\textup{ass:study-exchangeability}\) simultaneously gives\[V_t^-(X;\gamma)\le q_{t0}(X)\le V_t^+(X;\gamma)\qquad P_X\text{-almost surely}.\]Define the proof intermediates\[L_t(X;\theta)=\max\{V_t^-(X;\gamma),W_t^-(X;\rho)\},\qquad U_t(X;\theta)=\min\{V_t^+(X;\gamma),W_t^+(X;\rho)\}.\]The two interval inclusions give \(L_t(X;\theta)\le q_{t0}(X)\le U_t(X;\theta)\) almost surely. Consequently, \((L_t-U_t)_+=0\) almost surely and \(E_{P_X}[(L_t-U_t)_+]=0\) for both treatment arms. Definition \(\textup{def:compatibility-functional}\) now gives \(C_P(\theta)=0\), contradicting \(C_P(\theta)>0\). Hence \(\mathcal M(P,\rho,\gamma)=\varnothing\).

snapshot.stmt: Under \(\textup{ass:uniform-study-positivity}\) and \(\textup{ass:uniform-treatment-positivity}\), \(C_P(\theta)>0\) implies \(\mathcal M(P,\rho,\gamma)=\varnothing\). Indeed, for any \(Q\in\mathcal M(P,\rho,\gamma)\), the weighted observational-mixture identity may be divided by \(1-\pi(X)\ge\varepsilon\). Since \(c_t\in[C_t^-,C_t^+]\), it follows that \(q_{t0}\in[W_t^-,W_t^+]\), while study exchangeability gives \(q_{t0}\in[V_t^-,V_t^+]\). Thus every nonnegative conditional violation is zero almost surely, contradicting \(C_P(\theta)>0\).

assumptions.ass:uniform-treatment-positivity: \(\varepsilon\le e_t(X,s)\le1-\varepsilon\) \(P\)-almost surely for every \(t\in\mathcal T\) and \(s\in\{0,1\}\).

prop:two-stratum-cancellation.proof_tex: Under \(P^{\mathrm{can}}\), every listed mean belongs to \([1/4,8]\). For \(m\in[1/4,8]\), \(\textup{def:endpoint-kernel}\) gives \(\nu_m\) nonnegative weights summing to one, support contained in \(\{1/4,8\}\), and mean \(m\); hence every displayed observed outcome kernel is valid. Moreover,\[\pi(x_j)=\sum_{t\in\mathcal T}P^{\mathrm{can}}(S=1,A=t\mid X=x_j)=\frac12,\]and, for every \(s,t,j\),\[e_t(x_j,s)=\frac{P^{\mathrm{can}}(S=s,A=t\mid X=x_j)}{P^{\mathrm{can}}(S=s\mid X=x_j)}=\frac{1/4}{1/2}=\frac12.\]At \(\rho=\gamma=1\), the published endpoints in \(\textup{def:published-endpoints}\) reduce to\[v_t^-=0,\qquad v_t^+=2\mu_{1t},\qquad w_t^-=\frac12\mu_{0t},\qquad w_t^+=\frac32\mu_{0t}.\]For arm \(0\), the trial and observational intervals at \(x_1\) are therefore \([0,1]\) and \([2,6]\), so\[H_0^{\mathrm{LRVP}}(x_1;(1,1))=\min\{1,6\}-\max\{0,2\}=-1.\]At \(x_2\) they are \([0,8]\) and \([2,6]\), so \(H_0^{\mathrm{LRVP}}(x_2;(1,1))=6-2=4\). It follows that\[G_0^{\mathrm{LRVP}}(1,1)=\frac45(-1)+\frac15(4)=0.\]For arm \(1\), both strata have published intervals \([0,2]\) and \([1/2,3/2]\), whence \(H_1^{\mathrm{LRVP}}=1\) pointwise and \(G_1^{\mathrm{LRVP}}(1,1)=1\). For the support-clipped endpoints in \(\textup{def:exact-endpoints}\), the equalities \(1-\rho=1-\gamma=0\) give \(C_t^-=V_t^-=1/4\), whereas \(C_t^+=\min\{8,2\mu_{0t}\}\) and \(V_t^+=\min\{8,2\mu_{1t}\}\). Thus, for arm \(0\),\[V_0(x_1)=[1/4,1],\qquad V_0(x_2)=[1/4,8],\qquad C_0(x_j)=[1/4,8].\]Since \(e_0=e_1=1/2\) and \(\mu_{00}=4\), both strata have\[W_0(x_j)=\left[\frac12(4)+\frac12\left(\frac14\right),\frac12(4)+\frac12(8)\right]=[17/8,6].\]For arm \(1\), both \(\mu_{01}\) and \(\mu_{11}\) equal one, and hence\[V_1(x_j)=C_1(x_j)=[1/4,2],\qquad W_1(x_j)=\left[\frac12(1)+\frac12\left(\frac14\right),\frac12(1)+\frac12(2)\right]=[5/8,3/2].\]The positive gap between the maximum lower endpoint and the minimum upper endpoint is therefore \(17/8-1=9/8\) for arm \(0\) at \(x_1\), zero for arm \(0\) at \(x_2\), and zero for arm \(1\) at either stratum. Definition \(\textup{def:compatibility-functional}\) yields\[C_{P^{\mathrm{can}}}(1,1)=\max\left\{\frac45\frac98+\frac15(0),0\right\}=\frac9{10}.\]Finally, since \(\varepsilon<1/2\), the equalities \(\pi=e_t=1/2\) satisfy both uniform positivity assumptions. Proposition \(\textup{prop:positive-violation-infeasibility}\) therefore applies and gives \(\mathcal M(P^{\mathrm{can}},1,1)=\varnothing\), even though the published arm-zero signed average equals zero.

snapshot.stmt: For \(P^{\mathrm{can}}\) at \(\theta=(1,1)\), all means lie in \([1/4,8]\), \(\pi(x_j)=e_t(x_j,s)=1/2\), and all observed kernels are valid. For arm \(0\), the published un-clipped intervals are \([0,1]\) and \([2,6]\) at \(x_1\), giving \(H_0^{\mathrm{LRVP}}(x_1)=-1\), and \([0,8]\) and \([2,6]\) at \(x_2\), giving \(H_0^{\mathrm{LRVP}}(x_2)=4\). Hence \(G_0^{\mathrm{LRVP}}=(4/5)(-1)+(1/5)4=0\). For arm \(1\), the published intervals are \([0,2]\) and \([1/2,3/2]\) at both strata, so \(H_1^{\mathrm{LRVP}}=G_1^{\mathrm{LRVP}}=1\). The support-clipped arm-zero intervals are \(V_0=[1/4,1]\), \(C_0=[1/4,8]\), and \(W_0=[17/8,6]\) at \(x_1\), and \(V_0=[1/4,8]\), \(C_0=[1/4,8]\), and \(W_0=[17/8,6]\) at \(x_2\). For arm \(1\), they are \(V_1=[1/4,2]\), \(C_1=[1/4,2]\), and \(W_1=[5/8,3/2]\) at both strata. Thus the only conditional violation is \(9/8\) at \(x_1\) for arm \(0\), so \(C_{P^{\mathrm{can}}}(1,1)=9/10\) and \(\mathcal M(P^{\mathrm{can}},1,1)=\varnothing\) by Proposition 1.

oeq:uniform-contact-inference.proof_tex: The proved \(\textup{thm:tie-local-separation-obstruction}\) shows that, in the canonical positive-mass endpoint-and-arm-tie subexperiment, the sharp uniform separation order is \(n^{-2\beta/(4\beta+d)}\), while \(\textup{lem:regular-constant-path-power}\) gives the pointwise \(n^{-1/2}\) Gaussian boundary along fixed constant directions. Thus no uniformly level procedure can have a nontrivial worst-case power curve indexed only by \(\sqrt n C_P(\theta)\) over the full tie-inclusive class. The additional \(\textup{lem:scalar-face-tangent-nonfactorization}\) proves that the displayed scalar multiplier input is insufficient to determine the tie derivative: two paths with identical integrated face derivatives have compatibility derivatives \(0\) and \(1/4\). Uniform coverage and uniform null level remain logically possible only after a selector-complete function-valued procedure and its missing uniform approximation and inversion arguments are supplied.

snapshot.stmt: Under \(P\in\overline{\mathcal P}\), \(\textup{ass:iid}\), and the construction-performance conditions in \(\textup{def:inference-handle}\), can the numerical directional-delta contact-set multiplier procedure attain \(\liminf_{n\to\infty}\inf_{P\in\overline{\mathcal P}}P(\mathcal K(P)\subseteq\widehat{\mathcal K}_{n,1-\alpha})\ge1-\alpha\) and yield uniformly level-\(\alpha\) tests of \(C_P(\theta)=0\), including positive-\(P_X\)-mass endpoint and arm ties?

With independent mean-zero unit-variance multipliers, put \(\mathbb G_n^\xi=n^{-1/2}\sum_i\xi_i\widehat\psi_i\), choose \(\delta_n\downarrow0\) with \(n^{-1/2}/\delta_n\to0\) and \((r_{\pi,n}+r_{e,n}+r_{\mu,n})/\delta_n\to0\), and set \(\widehat C_n^\xi=[\mathfrak F(\widehat\eta_n+\delta_n\mathbb G_n^\xi)-\mathfrak F(\widehat\eta_n)]/\delta_n\). Take deterministic \(\Theta_n\subset[0,1]^2\) with mesh \(h_n\to0\) and interpolation error \(o(n^{-1/2})\). Let \(\widehat c_{1-\alpha}\) be the conditional \((1-\alpha)\)-quantile of \(\sup_{\theta\in\Theta_n}\widehat C_n^\xi(\theta)\), and define \(\widehat{\mathcal K}_{n,1-\alpha}=\{\theta\in\Theta_n:\sqrt n\widehat C_n(\theta)\le\widehat c_{1-\alpha}\}\), enlarged by measurable closed grid cells in \(\Theta\). Hong--Li supplies the finite-difference derivative, Fang--Santos the directional map, Chernozhukov--Lee--Rosen contact selection, and cross-fitting nuisance linearization.

assumptions.ass:density-bounds: \(\underline f\le f_X(x)\le\bar f\) for Lebesgue-almost every \(x\in\mathcal X\).
assumptions.ass:uniform-treatment-positivity: \(\varepsilon\le e_t(X,s)\le1-\varepsilon\) \(P\)-almost surely for every \(t\in\mathcal T\) and \(s\in\{0,1\}\).
assumptions.ass:holder-nuisances: \(\pi\in\mathcal H_B^\beta(\mathcal X)\), \(e_t(\cdot,s)\in\mathcal H_B^\beta(\mathcal X)\), and \(\mu_{st}\in\mathcal H_B^\beta(\mathcal X)\) for every \(t\in\mathcal T\) and \(s\in\{0,1\}\).
